value: Examine work hardening in polycrystalline Fe–Al alloys with Al content from 15 to 35 at.%, focusing on dislocation structures, ordering, and elastic anisotropy.

Work hardening rate peaks in the range 26–29 at.% Al, maximum at ~28% Al.
The high work hardening is explained by the combination of superdislocation dipole hardening and the predominance of straight screw dislocations due to strong Peierls (core) effects.
Elastic anisotropy peaks near Fe–25–30% Al, but the authors argue that Peierls forces, not anisotropy itself, are responsible for the screw dislocation alignment and high work hardening.
Addition of 0.1% B caused a slight increase in flow stress but no consistent change in work hardening rate.
A small ductility peak at ~28% Al is attributed to the high work hardening rate meeting the Considère criterion.

Fig. 3 APB energy data from Ray–Crawford–Cockayne: APBI in DO3 order decreases from 78 to 65 mJ/m² over 25.5–28% Al, APBII 78–65 mJ/m² over 26–29% Al, APBI in B2 increases from 65 to 110 mJ/m² over 31–36% Al.
Fig. 4 elastic stiffness data show C44 increase and (C11-C12)/2 decrease near 25–30% Al, leading to high Zener anisotropy factor A.
Dislocation reactions forming sessile <100> edge dislocations were observed in Fe–28% Al and Fe–30% Al, contributing to additional hardening.
